---
title: "MoCap4ROS2"
description: "Production-ready ROS 2 C++ packages for streaming Vicon and OptiTrack motion-capture data into the PX4 EKF for indoor quadrotor experiments."
categories: [ROS 2, C++, Robotics, PX4, Motion Capture]
---

::: {.d-flex .gap-2 .flex-wrap .mb-3}
[Vicon4PX4 on GitHub](https://github.com/evannsm/vicon4px4){.btn .btn-sm .btn-primary target="_blank"}
[OptiTrack4PX4 on GitHub](https://github.com/evannsm/optitrack4px4){.btn .btn-sm .btn-primary target="_blank"}
[GaTech IFL Codebase](https://gtae.gitbook.io/ifl/ifl-codebase/px4+ros2-vicon-receiver){.btn .btn-sm .btn-outline-primary target="_blank"}
:::

<!-- Running indoor flight experiments means solving the same infrastructure problem every time: getting motion-capture position data into the drone's state estimator reliably. **Vicon4PX4** and **OptiTrack4PX4** are two companion ROS 2 C++ packages that handle the entire pipeline — coordinate frame conversion, EKF fusion, and full-state relay — so you can focus on your controller, not the plumbing. -->

Both packages share the same architecture and provide an identical downstream interface. The only difference is how they connect to the motion-capture system: Vicon4PX4 uses the DataStream SDK (fully vendored — no system install required), while OptiTrack4PX4 uses the NatNet protocol to connect to OptiTrack's Motive software.

Vicon4PX4 has been adopted and showcased by **Georgia Tech's Indoor Flight Lab (IFL)** as part of its [official codebase](https://gtae.gitbook.io/ifl/ifl-codebase/px4+ros2-vicon-receiver).

---

### The Problem

PX4's EKF expects external-vision data in a very specific format: NED coordinates, a particular quaternion component ordering, microsecond timestamps, and a `VehicleOdometry` message on the right topic. Every lab doing indoor flight ends up writing bespoke glue code to get there — and it breaks whenever someone upgrades PX4, switches motion-capture vendors, or moves to a new ROS 2 distro. These packages solve that once and for all.

<!-- ### Data Pipeline -->

Both packages follow the same three-stage pipeline:

```
MoCap System (ENU, mm)
  → Client Node (ENU→NED, mm→m, publishes PoseStamped)
    → Visual Odometry Relay (quat reorder, µs timestamps, 35 Hz)
      → /fmu/in/vehicle_visual_odometry → PX4 EKF2
        → Full-State Relay (merges EKF outputs, 40 Hz)
          → /merge_odom_localpos/full_state_relay
```

The **client node** connects to the motion-capture system, converts ENU measurements to NED, and publishes pose data as both quaternions and Euler angles under the rigid body name defined in the MoCap software.

The **visual odometry relay** takes the processed poses, reorders quaternion components to match PX4's convention, applies microsecond timestamps, and publishes `VehicleOdometry` messages at 35 Hz on `/fmu/in/vehicle_visual_odometry` for direct EKF external-vision fusion.

The optional **full-state relay** merges the fused EKF output from `/fmu/out/vehicle_odometry` and `/fmu/out/vehicle_local_position` into a single topic at 40 Hz, giving controllers and loggers convenient access to all pose data including higher-order derivatives from the EKF.

### Key Features

- **ENU → NED coordinate conversion** with millimeters-to-meters scaling
- **Pose published as both quaternions and Euler angles** under the rigid body name
- **PX4-compatible `VehicleOdometry`** messages with correct quaternion ordering and timestamps
- **Full-state relay** merging fused EKF outputs into a single topic for control and logging
- **Multiple launch modes**: client only, client + relay, or all nodes including full-state merger
- **Configurable networking**: set the MoCap system IP and the bridge handles the rest
- **Vendored SDKs**: Vicon DataStream SDK and NatNet SDK are bundled per-architecture — no system installs

<!-- ### Quick Start

Both packages use the same workspace structure:

```bash
# Source ROS 2
source /opt/ros/$ROS_DISTRO/setup.bash

# Create workspace
mkdir -p ~/ws_mocap_px4_msgs_drivers/src && cd ~/ws_mocap_px4_msgs_drivers/src

# Clone packages (use vicon4px4 or optitrack4px4 depending on your system)
git clone git@github.com:evannsm/vicon4px4.git       # or optitrack4px4
git clone -b v1.16_minimal_msgs git@github.com:evannsm/px4_msgs.git
git clone git@github.com:evannsm/mocap_msgs.git
git clone git@github.com:evannsm/mocap_px4_relays.git

# Install dependencies and build
cd ~/ws_mocap_px4_msgs_drivers
rosdep install --from-paths src --rosdistro $ROS_DISTRO -y --ignore-src
python3 -m colcon build --symlink-install \
  --cmake-args -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DCMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS=ON
source install/setup.bash
```

Then launch with one of the available launch files:

```bash
# Vicon
ros2 launch vicon4px4 client.launch.py
ros2 launch mocap_px4_relays visual_odometry_relay.launch.py
ros2 launch mocap_px4_relays full_state_relay.launch.py  # optional

# OptiTrack (also offers combined launch files)
ros2 launch optitrack4px4 client.launch.py
ros2 launch optitrack4px4 client_and_visual_odometry.launch.py
ros2 launch optitrack4px4 client_vision_full_all.launch.py
```

### OptiTrack Configuration

OptiTrack4PX4 exposes additional parameters for NatNet connection tuning via `config/optitrack4px4_params.yaml`:

| Parameter | Description |
|---|---|
| `connection_type` | Unicast or Multicast |
| `server_address` | IP of the Motive machine |
| `local_address` | IP of the Ubuntu machine |
| `server_command_port` | NatNet command port (default 1510) |
| `server_data_port` | NatNet data port (default 1511) |
| `map_xyz` / `map_rpy` | Axis remapping for non-standard setups | -->

### Compatibility

| | Vicon4PX4 | OptiTrack4PX4 |
|---|---|---|
| **ROS 2** | Jazzy / Humble | Jazzy / Humble |
| **Ubuntu** | 22.04 / 24.04 | 22.04 / 24.04 |
| **Architecture** | x86_64, ARM64 | x86_64 |
| **SDK** | Vicon DataStream 1.12 (vendored) | NatNet 1.12 (vendored) |
| **Build** | C++17, ament_cmake | C++17, ament_cmake |
| **License** | GPL-3.0 | GPL-3.0 |

**Built with:** ROS 2 (Jazzy / Humble) · C++17 · PX4 · Ubuntu 22.04 / 24.04 · x86\_64 & ARM64
